progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Review Feedback

This paper addresses an important and timely problem—distribution shift in deep learning—and proposes using data augmentation to simulate and train across multiple distributions simultaneously, drawing on ideas from multi-task learning (notably a Lookahead-based task grouping search). However, the paper suffers from significant issues in framing, clarity, and scientific contribution that undermine its suitability for a top-tier venue. Conceptually, the core idea—that training on a distribution improves test performance on that distribution—is largely a restatement of the standard i.i.d. assumption, and the paper does not adequately differentiate its "Multi-Distribution Learning" framework from well-established practices such as data augmentation, domain generalization, and robustness training (e.g., AugMix, DeepAugment, AdvProp). The novelty appears to lie primarily in applying an existing task-grouping algorithm (Fifty et al., 2021) to augmentation-induced distributions, which is an incremental contribution. Clarity is hindered by imprecise terminology (e.g., conflating "corruptions" with genuine distribution shift, loose use of "distribution," and an underspecified "Distribution Generalization Metrics" framework that is announced but not concretely defined in the introduction), and the writing contains redundancies and awkward phrasings. Methodologically, key concerns include: (1) experiments appear limited to CIFAR-10 with synthetic augmentations, which is insufficient to substantiate claims about real-world distribution shift, especially given that WILDS and similar benchmarks are explicitly cited; (2) the "zero-shot distribution inference" evaluation on held-out augmentations tests generalization to a narrow, artificial notion of shift; and (3) baselines seem limited to RandAugment and a no-augmentation model, omitting stronger robustness-oriented baselines. Overall, the paper would benefit from sharper positioning relative to prior work, more rigorous and diverse empirical evaluation (including real distribution-shift benchmarks), and a clearer articulation of what is genuinely new beyond repackaging augmentation-based training under a new name.

comments.title: Grammatical/logical error in opening definition of Distribution Shift
comments.quote: Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning.
comments.explanation: The sentence defines Distribution Shift as 'the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set.' But Distribution Shift is not a performance—it is a condition or phenomenon where the test distribution differs from the training distribution (as correctly stated in the abstract: 'Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution'). So the introduction's definition contradicts the abstract's definition. This is not merely a typo—as written, it conflates the phenomenon with the resulting performance gap. A careful reader could be misled about what Distribution Shift actually means in this paper. Severity is minor because the abstract already gives the correct definition and most readers will reconcile it.

comments.title: Claim of 'weighted average' metric not supported by what is reported
comments.quote: These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims the Average Distribution Accuracy / Distribution Generalization Metrics are 'a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.' However, later in the introduction the authors explicitly state: 'Rather than the naive average accuracy, which we have reported in these experiments, we want a metric that rewards covering many distributions and the diversity of these distributions.' This reveals that the weighted metric combining all three factors has NOT been implemented in this paper—only plain average accuracy is used. The intro's framing therefore overstates what the paper actually delivers.

comments.title: Unresolved citation marker '?' in related work
comments.quote: **?** utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.
comments.explanation: The '?' indicates a broken/missing BibTeX citation. It leaves a substantive claim unattributed, making the reader unable to verify the reference to what is presumably Geirhos et al. on texture bias (which is actually cited elsewhere in the intro as Stylized-ImageNet).

comments.title: Claim of 'much better performance' than RandAugment is previewed without qualification
comments.quote: We find much better performance with this cluster compared to a strong RandAugment baseline, which is not completely Zero-Shot, as well as a weak baseline of a model trained without any Data Augmentation.
comments.explanation: The parenthetical 'which is not completely Zero-Shot' is an important caveat: RandAugment's augmentation pool likely overlaps with the held-out test distribution, so the baseline isn't a fair Zero-Shot comparison. The introduction acknowledges this briefly but doesn't clarify what augmentations RandAugment uses vs. the held-out test distribution. Comparing a non-Zero-Shot baseline to a Zero-Shot method and then concluding the Zero-Shot method is 'much better' is questionable.

comments.title: 'Average Distribution Score' vs 'Average Augmentation Score' terminology drift
comments.quote: However, after 1,000 epochs (roughly 20 hours of training with our computing setup described previously), the model achieves a fairly strong Average Augmentation Score
comments.explanation: The paper's contributions explicitly introduce the 'Average Distribution Score' as a new metric. In Section 3.2 and Table 2, the authors switch to 'Average AugScore' / 'Average Augmentation Score.' These appear to refer to the same quantity, but the inconsistent naming risks confusing a reader about whether these are identical metrics.

comments.title: Overstated claim of 'very strong Zero-Shot generalization' for Rotation and Translation
comments.quote: For Rotation and Translation, we find a very strong Zero-Shot generalization with the group.
comments.explanation: The zero-shot numbers are 72.4% (Rotate) and 71.9% (Translate), compared to N=1 specialization of 75.2% and 79.4% respectively. So zero-shot performance is close to specialized but not 'very strong' in absolute terms—it's notably below the 83.8% RandAugment achieves on original test. The claim is relative to the RandAugment baseline, but 'very strong' isn't quantitatively anchored.

comments.title: Inconsistency between reported Darker zero-shot performance and claimed 'similar performance' on Jigsaw
comments.quote: We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization.
comments.explanation: The text just said 'we ran into optimization difficulties with our run leaving out the Darker distribution and it achieves a very low accuracy'. Then immediately follows 'We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization.' A natural reading is that Jigsaw zero-shot also had low/poor performance. But Table 4 shows Jigsaw zero-shot = 65.2%, which outperforms the No Augmentation baseline (62.5%) and is close to RandAugment (69.5%). This is not 'similar' to the failed Darker run (45.5%). The sentence is ambiguous—'similar' might refer back to 'strong generalization' in the earlier sentence about Rotation/Translation, but sentence ordering suggests it refers to the Darker run.

comments.title: Claim that negative augmentation (Gamma Contrast) 'does not benefit' from cluster not well-supported by numbers
comments.quote: We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast, supporting our analysis that the negative augmentation does not benefit from this particular cluster of training distributions.
comments.explanation: Gamma Contrast zero-shot under the 3-Augmentations group = 62.6%; No Augmentation baseline = 54.2%. So the cluster actually improves Gamma Contrast performance by ~8 percentage points over training without augmentation. The phrase 'does not benefit' is therefore inaccurate as written. What may be meant is that GC doesn't benefit *as much* as augmentations in the positive cluster, or that RandAugment (72.6%) beats it. But the literal claim 'does not benefit' contradicts the numbers.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Review Feedback

This paper addresses an important and timely problem—distribution shift in deep learning—and proposes using data augmentation to simulate and train across multiple distributions simultaneously, drawing on ideas from multi-task learning (notably a Lookahead-based task grouping search). However, the paper suffers from significant issues in framing, clarity, and scientific contribution that undermine its suitability for a top-tier venue. Conceptually, the core idea—that training on a distribution improves test performance on that distribution—is largely a restatement of the standard i.i.d. assumption, and the paper does not adequately differentiate its "Multi-Distribution Learning" framework from well-established practices such as data augmentation, domain generalization, and robustness training (e.g., AugMix, DeepAugment, AdvProp). The novelty appears to lie primarily in applying an existing task-grouping algorithm (Fifty et al., 2021) to augmentation-induced distributions, which is an incremental contribution. Clarity is hindered by imprecise terminology (e.g., conflating "corruptions" with genuine distribution shift, loose use of "distribution," and an underspecified "Distribution Generalization Metrics" framework that is announced but not concretely defined in the introduction), and the writing contains redundancies and awkward phrasings. Methodologically, key concerns include: (1) experiments appear limited to CIFAR-10 with synthetic augmentations, which is insufficient to substantiate claims about real-world distribution shift, especially given that WILDS and similar benchmarks are explicitly cited; (2) the "zero-shot distribution inference" evaluation on held-out augmentations tests generalization to a narrow, artificial notion of shift; and (3) baselines seem limited to RandAugment and a no-augmentation model, omitting stronger robustness-oriented baselines. Overall, the paper would benefit from sharper positioning relative to prior work, more rigorous and diverse empirical evaluation (including real distribution-shift benchmarks), and a clearer articulation of what is genuinely new beyond repackaging augmentation-based training under a new name.

comments.title: Grammatical/logical error in opening definition of Distribution Shift
comments.quote: Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning.
comments.explanation: The sentence defines Distribution Shift as 'the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set.' But Distribution Shift is not a performance—it is a condition or phenomenon where the test distribution differs from the training distribution (as correctly stated in the abstract: 'Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution'). So the introduction's definition contradicts the abstract's definition. This is not merely a typo—as written, it conflates the phenomenon with the resulting performance gap. A careful reader could be misled about what Distribution Shift actually means in this paper. Severity is minor because the abstract already gives the correct definition and most readers will reconcile it.

comments.title: Claim of 'weighted average' metric not supported by what is reported
comments.quote: These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims the Average Distribution Accuracy / Distribution Generalization Metrics are 'a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.' However, later in the introduction the authors explicitly state: 'Rather than the naive average accuracy, which we have reported in these experiments, we want a metric that rewards covering many distributions and the diversity of these distributions.' This reveals that the weighted metric combining all three factors has NOT been implemented in this paper—only plain average accuracy is used. The intro's framing therefore overstates what the paper actually delivers. A reader encountering the abstract/intro could reasonably expect a novel composite metric to be presented, but only a simple average is used in the experiments. This is a minor overclaim since it's clarified later, but it's misleading in the intro.

comments.title: Vertical Flip described as mirroring along x-axis
comments.quote: The Vertical Flip (UD Flip) augmentation inversely mirrors the original image along the x-axis.
comments.explanation: Initially this seemed fine since Horizontal Flip is described as mirroring along the y-axis (which is correct: LR flipping reflects across a vertical/y-axis). By the same convention, Vertical Flip (UD flip) should mirror across the x-axis (horizontal axis). Checking the Horizontal Flip description: 'mirrors the original image along the y-axis' — that matches convention. So the Vertical Flip description 'mirrors...along the x-axis' is actually correct. The word 'inversely' is odd but not wrong. Not an issue on reflection.

comments.title: Unresolved citation marker '?' in related work
comments.quote: **?** utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.
comments.explanation: The '?' indicates a broken/missing BibTeX citation. While this is a formatting issue (which I'm told not to flag), it also leaves a substantive claim unattributed, making the reader unable to verify the reference to what is presumably Geirhos et al. on texture bias (which is actually cited elsewhere in the intro as Stylized-ImageNet). This is a moderate issue because it leaves a claim in the literature review unsupported, though readers can likely infer the intended reference.

comments.title: Claim of 'much better performance' than RandAugment is previewed without qualification
comments.quote: We find much better performance with this cluster compared to a strong RandAugment baseline, which is not completely Zero-Shot, as well as a weak baseline of a model trained without any Data Augmentation.
comments.explanation: The parenthetical 'which is not completely Zero-Shot' is an important caveat: RandAugment's augmentation pool likely overlaps with the held-out test distribution, so the baseline isn't a fair Zero-Shot comparison. The introduction acknowledges this briefly but doesn't clarify what augmentations RandAugment uses vs. the held-out test distribution. A reader could be misled into thinking this is a fair apples-to-apples Zero-Shot comparison when it isn't. This is a framing concern that undermines the strength of the claim — comparing a non-Zero-Shot baseline to a Zero-Shot method and then concluding the Zero-Shot method is 'much better' is questionable. However, in an introduction/overview this is acknowledged in text, so I rate it minor.

comments.title: UD Flip / LR Flip naming inconsistency between text and table
comments.quote: Throughout training the Rotation, Translation, and Crop Augmentations have the lowest performance, whereas Vertical and Horizontal Flipping (shown as UD Flip and LR Flip) have the highest score.
comments.explanation: The text says Vertical and Horizontal Flipping are the highest-scoring augmentations throughout training. Table 2, however, shows 'Max AugName' as UD Flip for epochs 10, 30, 50, 200, and then LR Flip for 350 and 1000. If UD Flip = Vertical Flip (as stated in Section 3 of the experimental setup: 'Vertical Flip (UD Flip)') then the text is consistent with the table. But later in Section 3.3 the authors write 'Vertical Flipping (UD Flip) causes a significant drop in performance across the other augmentations,' which is consistent. However, there is an internal inconsistency elsewhere: the passage says at end of training the model 'achieves a nearly identical score on the Horizontal Flip as the Original Test set' — Table 2 at epoch 1000 shows Max AugScore = 83.2% with Max AugName = LR Flip, and Original Test = 83.4%, which matches. So this checks out. The only concern remaining is that the claim 'Vertical and Horizontal Flipping ... have the highest score' throughout training is partially inaccurate: Table 2 only tracks the max augmentation at each epoch, and there is no direct evidence that Vertical Flip specifically scored near-top throughout — only that it was the max in early epochs. This is a minor overclaim but readers can resolve it from the table.

comments.title: Ambiguous sign convention in Lookahead gradient update description
comments.quote: We find a strong outlier result where a proposed gradient update on Vertical Flipping (UD Flip) causes a significant drop in performance across the other augmentations.
comments.explanation: The passage describes Figure 2 as showing 'the performance difference by taking the proposed gradient update,' but doesn't specify the sign convention (is a positive value a performance gain or loss? Is the y-axis the source of the update and x-axis the evaluation, as stated, or the reverse?). The authors restate the axis convention ('y-axis = proposer, x-axis = evaluated'), which is good. However, without seeing the figure, readers are told UD Flip causes a 'significant drop' — but Section 3.2 (Table 2) reported UD Flip as one of the HIGHEST-scoring test distributions. These facts are not contradictory (UD Flip being easy to classify doesn't mean training on it helps other distributions), but the juxtaposition could mislead a careful reader into thinking there is a contradiction. The authors could clarify this asymmetry explicitly. Minor ambiguity only.

comments.title: 'Average Distribution Score' vs 'Average Augmentation Score' terminology drift
comments.quote: However, after 1,000 epochs (roughly 20 hours of training with our computing setup described previously), the model achieves a fairly strong Average Augmentation Score
comments.explanation: The paper's contributions explicitly introduce the 'Average Distribution Score' as a new metric. In Section 3.2 and Table 2, the authors switch to 'Average AugScore' / 'Average Augmentation Score.' Reading carefully, these appear to refer to the same quantity — the mean accuracy over N augmentation-induced test distributions. But the inconsistent naming risks confusing a reader about whether these are identical metrics. A brief clarifying sentence or consistent naming would resolve this. This is a minor notation/terminology drift, not a technical error.

comments.title: Forward reference to Table 4 without context
comments.quote: Table 4 illustrates the Zero-Shot Distribution Inference of the grouping with a strong RandAugment baseline and a weak baseline of training with No Data Augmentation.
comments.explanation: The summary passage refers to Table 4 but in the visible passage we only see Tables 1 and 2 (and Table 3 is mentioned in section 3.4). This is a forward reference to content not shown here, which per the instructions should not be flagged. Not flagging as an issue — just noting that the reader would need to see Table 4 elsewhere.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about RTJ cluster's optimization difficulty vs. Table 3 results
comments.quote: We encountered an optimization difficulty with the Rotate, Translate, Jigsaw (abbreviated as RTJ in Table3) that achieves low performance.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 3, the row labeled '3 Augs(RTJ)' shows Rotate Test 37.3%, Translate Test 33.2%, N/A for Darker, Jigsaw Test 42.2%, Average 37.6%. But 'RTJ' in the text stands for Rotate, Translate, Jigsaw — which would mean Darker is the held-out distribution, and in Table 3 the held-out column should be N/A. Indeed the Darker column shows N/A, which is consistent. However, in Section 3.4's second paragraph, the text says 'we ran into optimization difficulties with our run leaving out the Darker distribution' — this matches RTJ (training on R, T, J, holding out D). So the naming is consistent. The concern is minor: the authors use RTJ both as the training set abbreviation and their Table 3 labeling seems correct. No real error, but readers may briefly be confused because the failed run corresponds to 'holding out Darker' in Table 4's cross-validation framing. This is an ambiguity rather than error.

comments.title: Zero-Shot claim about 3-Augmentations row conflates training-included vs held-out distributions
comments.quote: The entires for 3 Augmentations in Table 4 reflect the Zero-Shot accuracy without training on that augmented distribution.
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows '3 Augmentations' with entries for Rotate (72.4%), Translate (71.9%), Darker (45.5%), Jigsaw (65.2%), Gamma Contrast (62.6%). The text says each entry is the Zero-Shot accuracy where that distribution was held out from training. For Gamma Contrast, this is clearly zero-shot since GC was never in the positive cluster of 4. But the 4-distribution positive cluster is {Rotation, Translation, Dark, Jigsaw}. The cross-validation leaves out one of these four and trains on the remaining three. So Rotate's 72.4% = trained on {T, D, J}, Translate's 71.9% = trained on {R, D, J}, Darker's 45.5% = trained on {R, T, J}, Jigsaw's 65.2% = trained on {R, T, D}. Checking against Table 3: Rotate test with TDJ training = 72.4% ✓ matches. Translate with RDJ = 71.9% ✓ matches. Darker with RTJ = N/A in Table 3 but Table 4 reports 45.5% — this is the failed optimization run the authors mentioned. Jigsaw with RTD = Table 3 shows 'N/A' for Jigsaw column in RTD row — wait, Table 3's RTD row has N/A for Jigsaw test, but Table 4 shows 65.2%. Actually re-reading Table 3: '3 Augs(RTD)' row has Jigsaw Test N/A, meaning Jigsaw is held out of training (since training is R,T,D), so the Jigsaw test accuracy IS the zero-shot result, which should match Table 4's 65.2%. But Table 3 shows N/A, not 65.2%. This is inconsistent — the N/A seems to be used to mark 'held out from training' but the actual zero-shot accuracy should be reportable. The tables are inconsistent in how they treat held-out distributions: Table 3 marks them N/A (probably because that column is for distributions included in training), while Table 4 reports zero-shot numbers. This is a presentation issue but could confuse readers trying to cross-reference. Actually, this is by design: Table 3 is about 'Generalization to Test Distributions included in the Training Distribution' — so N/A for the held-out one makes sense, and Table 4 reports the zero-shot number for that held-out distribution. So the tables are complementary. Not a real error.

comments.title: Overstated claim of 'very strong Zero-Shot generalization' for Rotation and Translation
comments.quote: For Rotation and Translation, we find a very strong Zero-Shot generalization with the group.
comments.explanation: The zero-shot numbers are 72.4% (Rotate) and 71.9% (Translate), compared to N=1 specialization of 75.2% and 79.4% respectively (from Table 3). So zero-shot performance is close to specialized but not 'very strong' in absolute terms — it's notably below the 83.8% RandAugment achieves on original test. The claim is relative to the RandAugment baseline (57.4% and 49.7% on these tests), where the zero-shot group does significantly outperform. Given the comparison context, the claim is defensible but somewhat loose since 'very strong' isn't quantitatively anchored. Minor framing concern.

comments.title: Inconsistency between reported Darker zero-shot performance and claimed 'similar performance' on Jigsaw
comments.quote: We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization.
comments.explanation: The text just said 'we ran into optimization difficulties with our run leaving out the Darker distribution and it achieves a very low accuracy'. Then immediately follows 'We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization.' A natural reading is that Jigsaw zero-shot also had low/poor performance. But Table 4 shows Jigsaw zero-shot = 65.2%, which outperforms the No Augmentation baseline (62.5%) and is close to RandAugment (69.5%). This is not 'similar' to the failed Darker run (45.5%). The sentence is ambiguous — 'similar' might refer back to 'strong generalization' in the earlier sentence about Rotation/Translation, but sentence ordering suggests it refers to the Darker run. This ambiguity could mislead readers about which result Jigsaw is similar to. Moderate ambiguity.

comments.title: Claim that negative augmentation (Gamma Contrast) 'does not benefit' from cluster not well-supported by numbers
comments.quote: We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast, supporting our analysis that the negative augmentation does not benefit from this particular cluster of training distributions.
comments.explanation: Gamma Contrast zero-shot under the 3-Augmentations group = 62.6%; No Augmentation baseline = 54.2%. So the cluster actually improves Gamma Contrast performance by ~8 percentage points over training without augmentation. The phrase 'does not benefit' is therefore inaccurate as written. What may be meant is that GC doesn't benefit *as much* as augmentations in the positive cluster, or that RandAugment (72.6%) beats it. But the literal claim 'does not benefit' contradicts the numbers. Moreover, the text says 'We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast' — unclear what 'best' means here; there's only one reported number per method. This phrasing is confusing and the claim slightly overstates the negative-affinity story.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—robustness to distribution shift—and the basic empirical question of whether explicit augmentation with anticipated corruptions improves performance on corresponding shifted test distributions is relevant. However, the framing and contribution appear relatively weak and underdeveloped: much of the proposed “Multi-Distribution Learning” setup seems close to standard data augmentation, corruption robustness, and multi-domain training, but the paper does not yet clearly distinguish its novelty from existing work on augmentation policies, domain generalization, robustness benchmarks, or multi-task/data mixture learning. Several definitions are imprecise or problematic—for example, distribution shift is described as “performance” rather than a mismatch between train/test distributions, and “learning multiple distributions” is not formally specified beyond applying multiple corruptions. The motivation is understandable, but the writing is often broad and repetitive, with many capitalized terms used as if they were formal concepts before being rigorously defined. The proposed metrics and “distribution affinity” idea could be interesting, but the excerpt does not make clear why these metrics are necessary, how diversity is quantified, or whether the lookahead grouping method provides insight beyond selecting augmentations that empirically work well together. A major concern is that the experimental setup may be somewhat tautological: training on the same corruption family used at test time naturally improves performance on that corruption, so the paper must more convincingly demonstrate generalization to genuinely unseen shifts and compare against strong augmentation and robustness baselines. Overall, the paper has a reasonable empirical direction but needs sharper conceptual framing, stronger positioning relative to prior work, clearer definitions, and more compelling evidence that the proposed framework yields nontrivial distribution generalization rather than expected gains from supervised exposure to test-time corruptions.

comments.title: Distribution shift is defined as performance rather than a distributional mismatch
comments.quote: Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent here: they are motivating the empirical problem that models perform poorly when the test distribution differs from the training distribution. However, under the standard convention in robustness/domain-generalization work, distribution shift is the mismatch between training and test distributions, e.g. \(P_{\mathrm{train}}(X,Y) \neq P_{\mathrm{test}}(X,Y)\), while the performance degradation is a consequence or measurement of that shift. The abstract states this more accurately: “Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution.” Thus the concern is not resolved by context; the introduction redefines the term in a way that conflates the phenomenon with the evaluation outcome. This is easy to fix by saying something like “Distribution shift, where test distributions are not identical to the training distribution, often causes poor performance.”

comments.title: Main distribution-generalization metric is inconsistently named and underspecified
comments.quote: We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The paper alternates among “Average Distribution Accuracy,” “Average Distribution Score,” “Average Augmentation Score,” and “Distribution Generalization Score,” without clearly separating the current reported statistic from the proposed future metric. More importantly, the described metric appears to be a weighted combination of accuracy/loss, distribution count, and distribution diversity, but the experimental tables seem to report simple unweighted averages over selected augmented test accuracies. For example, Table 1’s Average Distribution Score for No Augmentation appears to be \((41.6 + 61.3)/2 = 51.45\%\), excluding the original test accuracy and not incorporating any diversity term. Later text also says the experiments report a “naive average accuracy” and frames the diversity-aware metric as future work. If the reported score is only a naive average, the contribution should not be presented as an already implemented coverage/diversity metric; if the richer metric is used, its formula, weighting, normalization, and definition of distribution diversity are missing.

comments.title: Lookahead grouping and zero-shot evaluation may use test or target-distribution information for model selection
comments.quote: We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets.
comments.explanation: The intended procedure is understandable: adapt a multi-task lookahead/task-affinity method and group augmentations whose gradient updates help one another. In standard practice, however, affinity estimation and grouping decisions should be based on training or validation data, not on final test sets, because the grouping is a model-selection decision. The wording explicitly says proposed updates are judged by performance “across both test sets,” and later text suggests the selected four augmentations and one negative comparison augmentation are chosen from this Lookahead search before the zero-shot experiments. If the same corrupted test sets or held-out target distributions are used to choose the high-affinity cluster, negative augmentation, or zero-shot training groupings, then subsequent test results are biased by test-set or target-distribution selection. This is especially important for the claimed “Zero-Shot Distribution Inference”: a held-out distribution may be absent from model training but still have influenced model design. The paper should clarify whether all affinity estimation and selection used separate validation data and whether each held-out zero-shot distribution was excluded from all model-selection decisions.

comments.title: Abstract phrasing could be read as including test examples in training
comments.quote: These experiments confirm that the accuracy of models evaluated on a test set included in the training distribution is much higher compared to test distributions not included in training.
comments.explanation: I think the authors mean that the training data are augmented using the same corruption family as the test data, not that the actual test examples are placed in the training set. That intent is consistent with the framework described later, where corruptions are applied separately to the original training data and original test data. Still, the phrase “test set included in the training distribution” is easy to misread as test-set leakage, especially because the paper repeatedly distinguishes training sets, test sets, and distributions. A clearer phrase would be “a test distribution whose corresponding augmentation was included during training.” This is mainly an ambiguity in wording, but it matters because the method’s validity depends on not training on the test examples themselves.

comments.title: Statement that experiments are limited to geometric transformations contradicts the listed augmentations
comments.quote: Our experiments utilize Data Augmentation to simulate diverse data distributions. We are currently limited to geometric transformations such as Rotations or Horizontal Flipping.
comments.explanation: The point seems to be that the study uses relatively simple hand-designed augmentations rather than more semantic domain translations. However, the later methods list includes Bright, Dark, Gamma Contrast, Cutout, and Jigsaw, which are not all geometric transformations in the usual sense. Brightness and gamma contrast are photometric/intensity transformations; Cutout is an occlusion/masking transformation; Jigsaw changes spatial arrangement but is not a standard geometric transform like rotation/translation/flip. Thus the limitation is misstated. A more accurate phrasing would be that the experiments are limited to simple image corruptions/augmentations, including geometric and photometric transformations.

comments.title: Augmentation procedures are not specified enough to reproduce the distributions
comments.quote: The Rotation augmentation rotates an image along polar coordinates between 0 to 360 degrees. The Translation augmentation shifts an image along the x and y axes. This is done by moving pixels over/up by x pixels and adding 0s to displaced values. The Crop augmentation selects a subset of the original image and then resizes the subset to the original resolution with bilinear interpolation. The Cutout augmentation selects patches of the image and replaces the patch with 0 values. The Bright augmentation adds k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 255 (white). The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 0 (black). The Gamma Contrast augmentation alters the distribution of pixel values to a smaller window between 0 and 255. The Horizontal Flip (LR Flip) augmentation mirrors the original image along the y-axis. The Vertical Flip (UD Flip) augmentation inversely mirrors the original image along the x-axis. The Jigsaw augmentation divides the image into n pixel windows and shuffles their location in the image.
comments.explanation: The authors provide a helpful qualitative description of each augmentation and identify imgaug as the implementation source. Still, the actual training/test distributions depend heavily on missing parameter choices: rotation angle sampling distribution, interpolation and padding mode, translation range, crop fraction/aspect ratio, number and size of Cutout patches, brightness offset k, gamma range, Jigsaw grid/window size n, whether parameters are fixed per image or resampled each epoch, and whether train/test corrupted distributions use deterministic or stochastic augmentation. Since the paper’s central claims concern learning specific augmentation-induced distributions, these parameters are not incidental implementation details; they define the distributions being learned. A reader could not reliably reproduce the experiments from the description as written.

comments.title: Training setup lacks key hyperparameters needed for reproducibility
comments.quote: Our experiments report training and evaluation strategies for the ResNet152V2 He et al. (2016) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiments are implemented using the Keras framework. The experiments are run on an NVIDIA A100 GPU in a Google Colab Pro+ runtime, with a maximum training time of roughly 20 hours.
comments.explanation: The hardware/framework information is useful, and later sections report some epoch counts. But the passage does not specify standard training details that substantially affect CIFAR-10 performance and multi-distribution comparisons: optimizer, learning rate and schedule, batch size, weight decay, initialization/pretraining status, input resizing/preprocessing for ResNet152V2, number of steps per epoch under alternating training, random seeds or number of runs, and RandAugment hyperparameters. Because several conclusions compare methods under “similar computation,” missing these details makes it difficult to assess whether differences are due to the distribution-learning scheme rather than optimization choices or training budget.

comments.title: Description of ImageNet-C/ImageNet-P is factually imprecise
comments.quote: Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019) presented ImageNet-C, a dataset constructed from a set of 75 Data Augmentations and ImageNet-P constructed from adversarial injections.
comments.explanation: The broader point—that corruption benchmarks reveal robustness failures—is correct. However, the standard description is that ImageNet-C consists of 15 corruption types at 5 severity levels, often yielding 75 corruption/severity combinations, not 75 distinct data augmentations. ImageNet-P consists of perturbation sequences for measuring prediction stability under small common perturbations; it is not usually characterized as being constructed from “adversarial injections.” This is a related-work precision issue rather than a core experimental flaw, but the current wording could mislead readers about what these benchmarks contain.

comments.title: Interpretation of Table 3 contains inconsistent or unsupported comparison claims
comments.quote: However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test.
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be that the selected grouping is more compute-efficient than training on all 10 augmentations, but the comparison is ambiguous and not numerically correct as written. Table 3 contains two different 10-augmentation references, at 100 epochs and 1000 epochs. Relative to the 100-epoch 10-augmentation model, the three successful clusters outperform it on all listed included tests, including Jigsaw, so the exception “other than the Jigsaw test” is unnecessary. Relative to the 1000-epoch 10-augmentation model, the exception is not limited to Jigsaw: for example, TDJ has Translate 72.4% versus 74.8%, and RDJ/TDJ have lower average scores than 74.5%. A related interpretation issue appears in the claim that “Darker and Jigsaw” benefit from the ensemble: Darker is supported by grouped rows exceeding the 1 Aug row, but grouped Jigsaw accuracies are below the 1 Aug Jigsaw value. The text should specify the intended compute-matched baseline and revise the Jigsaw/ensemble interpretation to match the table.

comments.title: Table 4 zero-shot results are under-specified and partly overinterpreted
comments.quote: The entires for 3 Augmentations in Table 4 reflect the Zero-Shot accuracy without training on that augmented distribution.
comments.explanation: For Rotation, Translation, Darker, and Jigsaw, each Table 4 entry appears to come from a different leave-one-distribution-out model, which is a reasonable cross-validation design. However, the table header “TrainingDistributions” with a single row labeled “3 Augmentations” could easily be read as one fixed model evaluated on all five test distributions. Gamma Contrast is especially under-specified: because it is outside the four selected augmentations, “without training on that augmented distribution” does not identify whether the model was trained on all four selected distributions, on one of the 3-of-4 subsets, or on the best of several subsets. This also affects the claim that the “best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast” supports the conclusion that the negative augmentation does not benefit from the cluster. The reported 62.6% is higher than the No Augmentation baseline of 54.2%, so it supports at most a weaker claim that Gamma Contrast benefits less than positively affiliated distributions or less than RandAugment, not that it does not benefit at all. The sentence “We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization” is also ambiguous: if compared to the preceding Darker result, Table 4 does not support it; if compared to the No Augmentation baseline, the comparison should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: RandAugment zero-shot status depends on unspecified augmentation set and parameters
comments.quote: Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The conceptual point is reasonable: if RandAugment’s operation pool contains transformations from the held-out test families, then it is not a clean zero-shot baseline in the same sense as holding out an augmentation family entirely. However, the paper does not specify here which RandAugment operation set, magnitude, number of operations, or implementation was used. This is important because standard RandAugment policies include operations such as rotation, translation, brightness/color, and contrast, but they do not necessarily include all distributions evaluated here, especially Jigsaw, and the exact “Darker” operation may depend on how brightness magnitudes and signs are sampled. Without those details, the claim that RandAugment has seen compositions relevant to “any of these distributions” is hard to verify, and the fairness of the zero-shot comparison is under-specified.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—robustness to distribution shift—and the basic empirical question of whether explicit augmentation with anticipated corruptions improves performance on corresponding shifted test distributions is relevant. However, the framing and contribution appear relatively weak and underdeveloped: much of the proposed “Multi-Distribution Learning” setup seems close to standard data augmentation, corruption robustness, and multi-domain training, but the paper does not yet clearly distinguish its novelty from existing work on augmentation policies, domain generalization, robustness benchmarks, or multi-task/data mixture learning. Several definitions are imprecise or problematic—for example, distribution shift is described as “performance” rather than a mismatch between train/test distributions, and “learning multiple distributions” is not formally specified beyond applying multiple corruptions. The motivation is understandable, but the writing is often broad and repetitive, with many capitalized terms used as if they were formal concepts before being rigorously defined. The proposed metrics and “distribution affinity” idea could be interesting, but the excerpt does not make clear why these metrics are necessary, how diversity is quantified, or whether the lookahead grouping method provides insight beyond selecting augmentations that empirically work well together. A major concern is that the experimental setup may be somewhat tautological: training on the same corruption family used at test time naturally improves performance on that corruption, so the paper must more convincingly demonstrate generalization to genuinely unseen shifts and compare against strong augmentation and robustness baselines. Overall, the paper has a reasonable empirical direction but needs sharper conceptual framing, stronger positioning relative to prior work, clearer definitions, and more compelling evidence that the proposed framework yields nontrivial distribution generalization rather than expected gains from supervised exposure to test-time corruptions.

comments.title: Distribution shift is defined as performance rather than a distributional mismatch
comments.quote: Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent here: they are motivating the empirical problem that models perform poorly when the test distribution differs from the training distribution. However, under the standard convention in robustness/domain-generalization work, distribution shift is the mismatch between training and test distributions, e.g. \(P_{\mathrm{train}}(X,Y) \neq P_{\mathrm{test}}(X,Y)\), while the performance degradation is a consequence or measurement of that shift. The abstract states this more accurately: “Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution.” Thus the concern is not resolved by context; the introduction redefines the term in a way that conflates the phenomenon with the evaluation outcome. This is easy to fix by saying something like “Distribution shift, where test distributions are not identical to the training distribution, often causes poor performance.”

comments.title: Average Distribution metric is described inconsistently and appears underspecified
comments.quote: We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The paper clearly wants a metric that rewards both accuracy and breadth/diversity of distributional coverage, which is a reasonable goal. But I could not reconcile this description with the rest of the provided context. The abstract and later tables refer to “Average Distribution Score,” while this sentence uses “Average Distribution Accuracy.” More importantly, the reported quantities in the experiments appear to be simple averages over accuracies on selected augmented test distributions; for example, in Table 1 the Average Distribution Score for No Augmentation is \((41.6 + 61.3)/2 = 51.45\%\), excluding the original test accuracy and not incorporating a diversity term or a weighting by number of distributions. Later context also says the experiments report a “naive average accuracy” and discusses a diversity-aware metric as future work. If the weighted metric is only a proposal, the prose here overstates its use; if it is actually used, the weighting formula, normalization, and definition of distribution diversity are missing. Because this metric is presented as a contribution, the inconsistency is more than cosmetic.

comments.title: Lookahead grouping appears to use test-set performance for model-selection decisions
comments.quote: We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets.
comments.explanation: The intended procedure is understandable: adapt a multi-task lookahead/task-affinity method and group augmentations whose gradient updates help one another. In standard practice, however, such affinity or grouping decisions should be based on training/validation data, not on the final test sets, because the grouping is a model-selection decision. The wording explicitly says the proposed update is judged by performance “across both test sets,” and the later context similarly describes the x-axis as evaluated “test distributions.” If the CIFAR-10 corrupted test sets are used to choose the four positive distributions and the negative comparison distribution, then subsequent reported test results on those same distributions are biased by test-set selection. This would be resolved if the authors used a held-out validation split or a separate transformed training/validation set for the lookahead analysis, but that is not stated here. Since the selected grouping is central to later claims, this is a potentially serious methodological issue.

comments.title: Abstract phrasing could be read as including test examples in training
comments.quote: These experiments confirm that the accuracy of models evaluated on a test set included in the training distribution is much higher compared to test distributions not included in training.
comments.explanation: I think the authors mean that the training data are augmented using the same corruption family as the test data, not that the actual test examples are placed in the training set. That intent is consistent with the framework described later, where corruptions are applied separately to the original training data and original test data. Still, the phrase “test set included in the training distribution” is easy to misread as test-set leakage, especially because the paper repeatedly distinguishes training sets, test sets, and distributions. A clearer phrase would be “a test distribution whose corresponding augmentation was included during training.” This is mainly an ambiguity in wording, but it matters because the method’s validity depends on not training on the test examples themselves.

comments.title: “Zero-shot” evaluation appears to use the held-out distributions during grouping selection
comments.quote: We select 4 augmentations from our Lookahead search to train together. We additionally select 1 augmentation with a negative affinity for the sake of comparison. We find that the grouping achieves a much higher Average Distribution Score than the N=10 model trained with similar computation. We then generalize these experiments to a more realistic evaluation of Distribution Shift. We pose this as a problem of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference. We propose that groupings with a high affinity to the novel test distribution will perform better. We use a cross-validation analysis to iteratively pair 3 out of the 4 augmented distributions for training, evaluating the held-out distribution Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended distinction: the model is not trained on the held-out augmented distribution in a given fold, so prediction on that distribution is “zero-shot” in the narrow sense of no supervised training examples from that distribution. However, the preceding sentences say the four augmentations are first selected using the Lookahead affinity search, and the stated hypothesis is that groupings with high affinity to the novel test distribution perform better. If the held-out distribution was included in the Lookahead search or in the selection of the high-affinity cluster, then the evaluation is not fully zero-shot with respect to distribution selection/model design; information about the target distribution has already been used. This matters because the claimed realistic setting would normally require selecting augmentations without access to the novel test distribution. The paper should clarify whether the held-out distribution was excluded from all affinity estimation and model-selection decisions, or else qualify the claim as “held-out from training” rather than fully zero-shot.

comments.title: Claimed new “Average Distribution Score” conflicts with later description as naive average accuracy
comments.quote: Finally, we propose directions to extend our Distribution Generalization Score. Rather than the naive average accuracy, which we have reported in these experiments, we want a metric that rewards covering many distributions and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The authors are right to separate the current empirical summary statistic from a more ambitious distribution-generalization metric. But this sentence creates tension with the contribution list immediately below, which says the paper presents a new metric, the Average Distribution Score. If the reported score is simply the unweighted average accuracy over selected test distributions, then it is not yet the richer metric described here as rewarding number and diversity of distributions. Conversely, if “Average Distribution Score” is intended to be the new metric, its formal definition and relation to the proposed future Distribution Generalization Score are unclear. This is not just naming: readers could reasonably infer that the headline metric already incorporates coverage/diversity, while the text says the experiments report only naive average accuracy.

comments.title: Terminology for the main metric is inconsistent
comments.quote: We find that the grouping achieves a much higher Average Distribution Score than the N=10 model trained with similar computation.
comments.explanation: I tried to resolve this by checking nearby usage. The passage alternates among “Average Distribution Score,” “Distribution Generalization Score,” and, in the broader context/tables, “Average Augmentation Score” or “Average Distribution Accuracy.” These may all be intended to denote the same unweighted average accuracy across augmented test distributions, but the paper also discusses a future metric that would include distribution number and diversity. Without a stable name and definition, it is hard to tell which quantity is being compared in claims such as this one. This is likely fixable by defining one current metric explicitly and reserving a different name for the proposed future generalized metric.

comments.title: Statement that experiments are limited to geometric transformations contradicts the listed augmentations
comments.quote: Our experiments utilize Data Augmentation to simulate diverse data distributions. We are currently limited to geometric transformations such as Rotations or Horizontal Flipping.
comments.explanation: The point seems to be that the study uses relatively simple hand-designed augmentations rather than more semantic domain translations. However, the later methods list includes Bright, Dark, Gamma Contrast, Cutout, and Jigsaw, which are not all geometric transformations in the usual sense. Brightness and gamma contrast are photometric/intensity transformations; Cutout is an occlusion/masking transformation; Jigsaw changes spatial arrangement but is not a standard geometric transform like rotation/translation/flip. Thus the limitation is misstated. A more accurate phrasing would be that the experiments are limited to simple image corruptions/augmentations, including geometric and photometric transformations.

comments.title: Augmentation procedures are not specified enough to reproduce the distributions
comments.quote: The Rotation augmentation rotates an image along polar coordinates between 0 to 360 degrees. The Translation augmentation shifts an image along the x and y axes. This is done by moving pixels over/up by x pixels and adding 0s to displaced values. The Crop augmentation selects a subset of the original image and then resizes the subset to the original resolution with bilinear interpolation. The Cutout augmentation selects patches of the image and replaces the patch with 0 values. The Bright augmentation adds k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 255 (white). The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 0 (black). The Gamma Contrast augmentation alters the distribution of pixel values to a smaller window between 0 and 255. The Horizontal Flip (LR Flip) augmentation mirrors the original image along the y-axis. The Vertical Flip (UD Flip) augmentation inversely mirrors the original image along the x-axis. The Jigsaw augmentation divides the image into n pixel windows and shuffles their location in the image.
comments.explanation: The authors provide a helpful qualitative description of each augmentation and identify imgaug as the implementation source. Still, the actual training/test distributions depend heavily on missing parameter choices: rotation angle sampling distribution, interpolation and padding mode, translation range, crop fraction/aspect ratio, number and size of Cutout patches, brightness offset k, gamma range, Jigsaw grid/window size n, whether parameters are fixed per image or resampled each epoch, and whether train/test corrupted distributions use deterministic or stochastic augmentation. Since the paper’s central claims concern learning specific augmentation-induced distributions, these parameters are not incidental implementation details; they define the distributions being learned. A reader could not reliably reproduce the experiments from the description as written.

comments.title: Training setup lacks key hyperparameters needed for reproducibility
comments.quote: Our experiments report training and evaluation strategies for the ResNet152V2 He et al. (2016) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiments are implemented using the Keras framework. The experiments are run on an NVIDIA A100 GPU in a Google Colab Pro+ runtime, with a maximum training time of roughly 20 hours.
comments.explanation: The hardware/framework information is useful, and later sections report some epoch counts. But the passage does not specify standard training details that substantially affect CIFAR-10 performance and multi-distribution comparisons: optimizer, learning rate and schedule, batch size, weight decay, initialization/pretraining status, input resizing/preprocessing for ResNet152V2, number of steps per epoch under alternating training, random seeds or number of runs, and RandAugment hyperparameters. Because several conclusions compare methods under “similar computation,” missing these details makes it difficult to assess whether differences are due to the distribution-learning scheme rather than optimization choices or training budget.

comments.title: Description of ImageNet-C/ImageNet-P is factually imprecise
comments.quote: Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019) presented ImageNet-C, a dataset constructed from a set of 75 Data Augmentations and ImageNet-P constructed from adversarial injections.
comments.explanation: The broader point—that corruption benchmarks reveal robustness failures—is correct. However, the standard description is that ImageNet-C consists of 15 corruption types at 5 severity levels, often yielding 75 corruption/severity combinations, not 75 distinct data augmentations. ImageNet-P consists of perturbation sequences for measuring prediction stability under small common perturbations; it is not usually characterized as being constructed from “adversarial injections.” This is a related-work precision issue rather than a core experimental flaw, but the current wording could mislead readers about what these benchmarks contain.

comments.title: Ambiguous and partly inconsistent comparison against the 10-augmentation scheme
comments.quote: However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a comparison between the three non-failed 3-augmentation clusters and the 10-augmentation baselines in Table 3. The intended point seems to be that the selected grouping is more compute-efficient than training on all 10 augmentations, which is consistent with the broader narrative. But the table contains two different 10-augmentation references, at 100 epochs and 1000 epochs, and the claim is not correct under either reading as written. Relative to the 100-epoch 10-augmentation model, the three successful clusters outperform it on all listed included tests, including Jigsaw, so the exception “other than the Jigsaw test” is unnecessary. Relative to the 1000-epoch 10-augmentation model, the exception is not limited to Jigsaw: e.g. TDJ has Translate 72.4% versus 74.8%, and RDJ/TDJ have lower average scores than 74.5%. The comparison likely just needs to specify the intended compute-matched baseline, but as stated the numerical claim is confusing.

comments.title: Claim that Jigsaw benefits from the ensemble is not supported by Table 3
comments.quote: We find an interesting division between distributions that benefit from specialization (Rotation and Translation) and others that benefit from the ensemble (Darker and Jigsaw).
comments.explanation: The specialization part is well supported for Rotation and Translation: the 1 Aug row gives 75.2% and 79.4%, higher than the corresponding grouped-training entries. Darker also plausibly benefits from grouped training, since the grouped rows reach 81.0–82.2% versus 74.9% for 1 Aug. However, Jigsaw does not show the same pattern in the selected 3-augmentation groupings: the grouped Jigsaw accuracies are 67.4% and 66.3%, both below the 1 Aug Jigsaw value of 71.6%. It is true that the 10 Augs, 1000 Epochs row reaches 76.0% on Jigsaw, but that is a different, much longer-trained model and does not directly support the claimed division in the grouping experiment. The claim may be intended to refer to a broader ensemble effect, but the evidence presented here does not cleanly establish it for Jigsaw.

comments.title: Table 4’s “3 Augmentations” row mixes cross-validation models and leaves Gamma Contrast training unspecified
comments.quote: The entires for 3 Augmentations in Table 4 reflect the Zero-Shot accuracy without training on that augmented distribution.
comments.explanation: The surrounding explanation helps: for Rotation, Translation, Darker, and Jigsaw, each Table 4 entry appears to come from a different leave-one-distribution-out model. That is a reasonable cross-validation design. However, the table header “TrainingDistributions” with a single row labeled “3 Augmentations” could easily be read as one fixed model evaluated on all five test distributions. More importantly, the Gamma Contrast entry is under-specified. Since Gamma Contrast is outside the four selected augmentations, “without training on that augmented distribution” does not identify whether the model was trained on all four selected distributions, on one of the 3-of-4 subsets, or on the best of several subsets. This matters because the text later says the best Gamma Contrast zero-shot accuracy is reported. A reader cannot reproduce or fairly compare the Gamma Contrast number without knowing which training distributions produced it and whether model selection used Gamma Contrast performance.

comments.title: Gamma Contrast result does not clearly support the claimed lack of benefit from the cluster
comments.quote: We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast, supporting our analysis that the negative augmentation does not benefit from this particular cluster of training distributions.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended logic: Gamma Contrast was identified as having negative affinity with the selected cluster, so zero-shot performance should be weaker than for positively related held-out distributions. But the reported number in Table 4 is 62.6%, which is higher than the No Augmentation baseline of 54.2%, so in an absolute baseline sense Gamma Contrast does appear to benefit somewhat from the cluster. It is lower than RandAugment’s 72.6%, but the paper itself argues RandAugment is not a truly zero-shot baseline. It is also unclear what “best” was selected over. Thus the result may support a weaker claim—Gamma Contrast benefits less than Rotation/Translation, or less than augmentations with positive affinity—but it does not straightforwardly support “does not benefit.”

comments.title: RandAugment zero-shot status depends on unspecified augmentation set and parameters
comments.quote: Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The conceptual point is reasonable: if RandAugment’s operation pool contains transformations from the held-out test families, then it is not a clean zero-shot baseline in the same sense as holding out an augmentation family entirely. However, the paper does not specify here which RandAugment operation set, magnitude, number of operations, or implementation was used. This is important because standard RandAugment policies include operations such as rotation, translation, brightness/color, and contrast, but they do not necessarily include all distributions evaluated here, especially Jigsaw, and the exact “Darker” operation may depend on how brightness magnitudes and signs are sampled. Without those details, the claim that RandAugment has seen compositions relevant to “any of these distributions” is hard to verify, and the fairness of the zero-shot comparison is under-specified.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about Zero-Shot Jigsaw performance
comments.quote: We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization.
comments.explanation: This sentence is difficult to interpret in context. Immediately before it, the authors discuss the Darker hold-out run, which achieved a very low 45.5% accuracy because of optimization difficulties. If “similar performance” refers to that preceding result, Table 4 does not support it: Zero-Shot Jigsaw is 65.2%, far above Darker’s 45.5%. If instead the intended comparison is to the No Augmentation baseline, then Jigsaw’s 65.2% is indeed only modestly above 62.5%, but that comparison is not stated. The ambiguity matters because Jigsaw is part of the selected high-affinity group, and the strength or weakness of its zero-shot generalization affects the interpretation of the grouping method.
